news.title: Luis de la Fuente reveals Spain will not man-mark Messi in World Cup final
news.lede: Coach tells of first encounter when Messi was 16. ‘I took man-marker off and Messi scored four’. Luis de la Fuente says he will not man-mark Lionel Messi in the World Cup final, even though the Argentina captain has scored eight times and provided four assists and the Spain coach’s own experience suggests it might not be a bad idea. In the buildup to Sunday’s final in New Jersey, De la Fuente told the story of the first time he encountered Messi, 22 years ago, when the Argentinian was a 16-year-old coming through at Barcelona, he was the youth-team coach at Sevilla and the under-19 cup draw brought them together in the last 16 in May 2004 at the Miniestadi.

news.title: Spanish Football Federation issue statement as training session suspended before World Cup final
news.lede: Adverse weather conditions have been something of a theme of the 2026 World Cup across North America – and that has proven no different heading into Sunday’s final. Though the situation has perhaps not played out quite as badly as was initially feared, England and France are among the countries to have had their games impacted by the weather. While there are reportedly few fears that Sunday’s final between Spain and Argentina won’t go ahead as planned amid poor air quality warnings, the weather in New Jersey has caused some disruption. Indeed, on Saturday, the Spanish Football Federation (RFEF) confirmed that their planned practice session at the Melanie Lane Training Ground had been suspended due to thunderstorms in the area.

news.title: Toni Kroos makes his World Cup final pick clear
news.lede: The 2026 World Cup is rapidly approaching its dramatic conclusion, and football's biggest legends have begun weighing in on who they believe will lift the trophy. Among the most anticipated opinions was that of Toni Kroos, a man who knows exactly what it takes to win a World Cup. The former Real Madrid midfielder, renowned for his tactical intelligence and trademark honesty, has now made it clear which team he believes holds the edge heading into the final. According to reports cited by Tribuna.com from Sky Sports, Kroos sees Spain as the favorite to win the World Cup.

news.title: ‘Where did they go?’: homeless people feel force of America’s brutality in World Cup clean-up
news.lede: Fifa says football brings the world together but the unhoused in Atlanta feel targeted and completely excluded from the tournament. “A lot of our community has been pushed out by the World Cup. We’re not just dollar signs, we’re more than that. We’re people and we’re frustrated that they’ve chosen to treat us less than human.”

news.title: Drake makes 7-figure bet on Argentina-Spain World Cup final
news.lede: Superstar rapper and singer Drake is pushing a ton of chips to the middle of the table ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup final between Argentina and Spain. As he revealed in a post on Instagram, Drake is dropping a whopping $1.5 million bet on the World Cup final. And he’s placing that bet on Argentina to win in regulation time. If Messi and Co. can emerge victorious at the end of stoppage time, Drake’s payout will be $5.1 million.

news.title: Didier Deschamps defends Michael Olise amid France World Cup fallout
news.lede: France’s disappointing World Cup semifinals defeat to Spain has brought the boo birds out of the woodwork. Les Bleus’ star-studded attack failed to shine for once, and players such as Bayern Munich’s Michael Olise were caught in the crosshairs of criticism — despite having enjoyed a brilliant tournament before the loss. It is all “a bit harsh”, according to France national team coach Didier Deschamps, who took to defending “He [Olise] wasn’t at his best against Spain, just like some of the others,” Deschamps relayed in comments captured by @iMiaSanMia. “He’s still got plenty of room to improve.

news.title: Why Tuchel, Ancelotti And Super Coaches Struggled At The World Cup
news.lede: Top club coaches like Tuchel and Ancelotti surprisingly faltered at the World Cup, while international specialists De la Fuente and Scaloni led their teams to the final. The article suggests that club "super coaches," accustomed to extensive player contact and control, struggled to adapt to the limited preparation time and unique demands of international tournaments. Their inability to quickly build team chemistry and implement intricate tactics, unlike in club football, proved to be a significant hurdle. In contrast, coaches with experience within national federations demonstrated a better understanding of how to foster an ethos and tactical message efficiently, highlighting that international success requires specific adaptability beyond club achievements.

news.title: Football vs. fútbol: Nine reasons I'll always take the NFL over World Cup | Opinion
news.lede: Eye of the beholder, etc., etc. For the fútbol faithful, the World Cup excitement is building to a crescendo ahead of Sunday's final between defending champion Argentina and Spain. But for the football faithful? We've been subjected to plenty.
